coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots/SnO2–Co3O4 composite for highly efficient electrochemical water oxidation
coredata.prism:issueName: Carbon Materials in Energy Storage and Conversion, Dedicated to the 5th Anniversary of the School of Energy at Soochow University
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Shunyan
Li, Chuanxi
Liu, Juan
Liu, Naiyun
Qiao, Shi
Han, Yuzhi
Huang, Hui
Liu, Yang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Nanocomposites based on transition metal oxides (Co3O4, etc.) and carbon nanostructures with low-cost, high activity and good stability are promising catalysts toward electrochemical water oxidation, which is desirable but remains challenging. Here, we report the design and synthesis of nanocomposite based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs), SnO2 and Co3O4 (CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4) as the electrocatalyst for highly efficient oxygen evolution reaction (OER). In alkaline media, the complex exhibited high electrocatalytic activity and long-term stability, which was better than either pristine Co3O4 or SnO2–Co3O4 composite. Moreover, the CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 with a molar ratio of Sn:Co at 1:3 revealed the highest catalytic activity toward OER among different molar ratios of CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 composites. Experimental results indicated that the Co atoms were considered as the active centre, the nano-sized SnO2 enhanced electronic conductivity and the insoluble CQDs layer on the surface effectively protected the catalyst, thus the composite structure resulted in the excellent electrocatalytic activity and high stability.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of control groups of SnO2–Co3O4, Co3O4 and SnO2

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology , the National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program) ( 2012CB825803 , 2013CB932702 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51422207 , 51132006 , 21471106 ), the Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education ( 20123201110018 ), a Suzhou Planning Project of Science and Technology ( ZXG2012028 ), and a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions ( PAPD ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the CQDs (the HRTEM image of a single CQD particle in inset). (b) Particle-size histogram of CQDs. (c) UV–vis absorption spectrum (black line) and Raman spectrum (red line) of CQDs. (d) The conductivity-titrant amount curve of CQDs. A and B are the equivalence points of carboxyl and hydroxyl, respectively. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) TEM image, (b) STEM image, and elemental mapping images of carbon, oxygen, tin and cobalt, (c) enlarged TEM image, and the inset was the FFT pattern and (d) HRTEM image of CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 (Sn:Co=1:3). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) XPS survey spectra of CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 (Sn:Co=1:3) and the high-resolution spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) Co 2p and (d) Sn 3d. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) XRD patterns of SnO2 (black trace), Co3O4 (red trace), CQDs (blue trace), SnO2–Co3O4 (green trace) and CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 (pink trace) (the latter two with Sn:Co=1:3). (b) FT-IR spectra of CQDs and CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 (Sn:Co=1:3) composite. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The OER activity of the CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 samples with various Sn:Co mole ratios (the potential was evaluated at a current density of 2mAcm−1; the current density was evaluated at 0.8V). (b) The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves of a bare RDE, CQDs, 20% Pt/C, SnO2, Co3O4, SnO2–Co3O4 and CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 (the latter two with Sn:Co=1:3) with a sweep rate of 10mVs−1 in 0.1M KOH solution. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Oxygen evolution CV curves of CQDs, SnO2, Co3O4, SnO2–Co3O4 and CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 (the latter two with Sn:Co=1:3) and bare GC in 0.1M KOH with a sweep rate of 20mVs−1. (b) Current transients recorded on the glassy carbon electrodes during ten sequential CV scans applied at CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 (Sn:Co=1:3) on the GC (black line) and at the bare GC substrate (blue line) in KOH at pH=13. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves and (b) Tafel plots of CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 (Sn:Co=1:3) catalyst recorded in 0.1 and 1M KOH solution. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves and (b) current–time (i–t) chronoamperometric response of three different sizes of nanocarbon [(1) ∼5, (2) ∼10, and (3) ∼100nm, respectively]/SnO2–Co3O4 (Sn:Co=1:3) and (4) the SnO2–Co3O4 (Sn:Co=1:3) samples at 0.57 V (vs. SCE) in 0.1M KOH solution for 6h. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic model of CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 (Sn:Co=1:3) nanocomposites for the elecotrocatalytic water oxidation. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots/SnO2–Co3O4 composite for highly efficient electrochemical water oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University, 199 Ren’ai Road, Suzhou 215123, Jiangsu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices
Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanocomposites based on transition metal oxides (Co3O4, etc.) and carbon nanostructures with low-cost, high activity and good stability are promising catalysts toward electrochemical water oxidation, which is desirable but remains challenging. Here, we report the design and synthesis of nanocomposite based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs), SnO2 and Co3O4 (CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4) as the electrocatalyst for highly efficient oxygen evolution reaction (OER). In alkaline media, the complex exhibited high electrocatalytic activity and long-term stability, which was better than either pristine Co3O4 or SnO2–Co3O4 composite. Moreover, the CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 with a molar ratio of Sn:Co at 1:3 revealed the highest catalytic activity toward OER among different molar ratios of CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 composites. Experimental results indicated that the Co atoms were considered as the active centre, the nano-sized SnO2 enhanced electronic conductivity and the insoluble CQDs layer on the surface effectively protected the catalyst, thus the composite structure resulted in the excellent electrocatalytic activity and high stability.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrocatalysts for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) are crucial for a variety of renewable energy applications and energy-intensive industries. The development of an efficient electrocatalyst with low onset potential and long-term stability for OER is constantly being pursued. Precious metal-based materials are dominant for OER owing to their high performance, including iridium (Ir) and ruthenium (Ru) oxides [1–3]. However, their high cost and scarcity limit their wide application [4,5]. Consequently, it is important to find efficient and cost-effective electrocatalysts for OER.

In recently years, extensive research efforts have been paid to the development of alternative OER electrocatalysts based on transition-metal oxides with sufficient activity and metal-free materials at a low cost [6–8]. Meanwhile, quite a few researchers have investigated the electrochemical performance of cobalt oxides and tin oxide. Cobalt oxides (Co3O4) and its substituted cobaltites have been demonstrated to be promising electrochemical materials with their good catalytic activity for OER in alkaline media [9,10]. But the limited current density, small surface area and low efficient electron-mass transfer hinder its practical application [11–13]. Tin oxide (SnO2) is identified as a good conductor of electricity used in electrochemistry and has received considerable attention over the past few decades [14–16]. The composites based on metal oxide and SnO2 can efficiently improve the electronic conductivity property [17]. Nevertheless, the poor cyclic stability in alkaline medium is a tricky problem for their further applications [18–20].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a novel class of nanocarbon, show small particle size (∼5nm), supernal conductivity, rapid electron transfer and electron reservoir properties [21–24]. The abundant functional groups on the surface of the CQDs as effective components make it much easier to form composites and further enhance the catalytic activities of the original materials [25–29]. Our previous study revealed that CQDs enhanced photocatalytic activity and structural stability of the Ag3PO4 complex photocatalysts, in which the CQDs layer on the surface of Ag3PO4 and Ag/Ag3PO4 particles could effectively protect Ag3PO4 from dissolution [28]. Recently, our group synthesized the novel CQDs/NiFe-LDH composite catalysts, which displayed good OER electrocatalytic activity with a high current and a small over potential in alkaline electrolytes [30]. In our structural design, the combination of CQDs with SnO2 and Co3O4 could effectively avert SnO2 dissolving in alkaline solution, leading to the structural stability owing to the insoluble CQDs layer on the surface of SnO2.

In this work, we reported the one-step fabrication of CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 composite by hydrothermal method. The new-type electrocatalyst showed efficient electrocatalytic activity for OER in alkaline medium. It was found that the insoluble CQDs layer on the surface of SnO2 could effectively protect SnO2, resulting in a long-term stability and significant improvements in the electrochemical performance. As a result, the obtained CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 composite demonstrated to be a high-efficiency OER catalyst, which outperformed either the pristine Co3O4 or the SnO2–Co3O4 composite.
All the chemicals used in this work were of analytical grade, used as obtained without further purification and commercially available unless otherwise stated.

In the experiment, the CQDs were synthesized by a facile electrochemical etching method reported by our group [30]. Two graphite rods (99.99%, Alfa Aesar Co. Ltd.) with a separation of 7.5cm were inserted into a certain amount of ultrapure water, one as the anode, and the other as the counter electrode. Then, it needed to apply the static potentials of 50V to the two electrodes using a direct current power. The anode graphite rod corroded and a dark-yellow solution occurred gradually in the big beaker after continuously stirring for 120h. To remove the precipitated small graphite particles and graphite oxide, the solution was filtered using slow-speed quantitative filter papers, followed by centrifuging at 22,000rpm for 25min. Finally, the water soluble CQDs solution was concentrated to 0.1mgmL−1 by rotary evaporation at 80°C. Through controlling the centrifugal rate (15,000rpm and 10,000rpm) of the untreated solution, the water soluble CQDs solution (∼10 and ∼100nm) was obtained by the electrochemical etching method.

Hydroxyl-group-free CQDs (∼5nm) solution was prepared by a simple treatment of the obtained water soluble CQDs. A flask (250mL) containing 0.1mL hydrazine hydrate and 120mL water soluble CQDs was refluxed with magnetic stirring for 2h at 100°C. Then, it needed to put the solution in a dialysis tube and dialyzed for two days. Finally, the hydroxyl-group-free CQDs were obtained.

The CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 was fabricated by a simple hydrothermal method at 120°C using CQDs, triethanolamine, Co(NO3)2·6H2O and SnCl4·5H2O as precursors, and urea served as dispersants. Firstly, Co(NO3)2·6H2O (0.435g, 1.5mmol), SnCl4·5H2O (0.175g, 0.5mmol), urea (0.090g) and triethanolamine (994μL, 6mmol) were dissolved in deionized water to form a uniform solution by stirring. After that, 10mL CQDs solution was injected into the mixed uniform solution. The mixture was violently stirred for 30min and then poured into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The hydrothermal treatment was set at 120°C for 24h. Until the autoclave cooled down to room temperature naturally, the CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4(Sn:Co=1:3) sample was collected by centrifugation (10,000rpm, 10min) and washed with deionized water for several times, then drying at 80°C for 6h. Finally, a light pink powder was obtained. Different mole ratios of SnCl4·5H2O and Co(NO3)2·6H2O were performed to obtain CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 with various SnO2 and Co3O4 contents. Detailed precursor compositions of synthesized samples were described in Table S1.

The hydroxyl-group-free CQDs (∼5nm)/SnO2–Co3O4, graphite/SnO2–Co3O4, carbon nanotube/SnO2–Co3O4 and graphene/SnO2–Co3O4 as electrocatalysts were prepared by similar procedure of CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4. ∼1mg of graphite (carbon nanotube or graphene) was injected into the solution instead of CQDs solution. The following process was the same as above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of control groups of SnO2–Co3O4, Co3O4 and SnO2
ce:section.ce:para: All controllable contrast samples were prepared by similar method of CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 (Sn:Co=1:3) synthesis. The Co3O4–SnO2 was synthesized with Co(NO3)2.6H2O (0.435g, 1.5mmol), SnCl4·5H2O (0.175g, 0.5mmol), urea (0.090g) and triethanolamine (994μL, 6mmol) (without a CQD precursor). The Co3O4 sample was synthesized with Co(NO3)2·6H2O (0.145g, 0.5mmol) and urea (0.090g). The SnO2 sample was synthesized with SnCl4·5H2O (0.175g, 0.5mmol) and triethanolamine (994 μL, 6mmol).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained with a FEI-Tecnai F20 (200kV) transmission electron microscope. The TEM samples were prepared by dropping the solution onto a copper grid with polyvinyl formal support film and dried in air. Raman spectra were collected on an HR 800 Raman spectroscope (J Y, France) equipped with a synapse CCD detector and a confocal Olympus microscope. The crystal structure of the resultant products was characterized by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) by using an X‘Pert-ProMPD (Holand) D/max-γA X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ =0.154178nm). The spectrograph uses 600gmm−1 gratings and a 633nm He-Ne laser. UV–vis absorption was recorded on a Lambda 750 (Perking Elmer) spectrophotometer in the wavelength range of 200–1000nm. The Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra of CQDs were obtained with a Varian Spectrum GX spectrometer. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained by using a KRATOS Axis Ultra-DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with a monochromatised Al Kα X-ray (hν =1486.6eV).

A usual three-electrode electrochemical cell was employed to measure the electrocatalytic performance, which was connected to an electrochemical workstation (CHI 920C, CH Instrument, Shanghai) coupled with a rotating disk electrode (RDE) system (ATA-1B, Jiangfen electroanalytical Instrument Co. Ltd., China). The rotating disk working electrode of glassy carbon (3mm diameter) loaded with catalyst was used as the working electrode. A platinum wire and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) were used as counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. The synthesized solid sample was grinded into powder in a mortar, and then dispersed in deionized water, reaching a concentration of 2g/L. After that, the mixture was treated with ultrasonication to generate a homogeneous solution. Next, the dispersion (5μL) was dropped onto the glassy carbon disk followed covering with 4μL of Nafion solution (0.5wt%). Finally, the treated glassy carbon electrode was dried at room temperature.

Different alkaline medium (0.1M KOH, pH∼13; 1M KOH, pH∼13.8) were degassed through bubbling nitrogen for 0.5h before investigating the OER activity. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves were measured with the sweep rate of 20mVs−1, 10mVs−1, respectively, and 5mVs−1 for Tafel plots. The RDE measurement was conducted with a rotation rate of 1600rpm.

In this work, the high-purity CQDs were synthesized by a facile electrochemical etching approach. In Fig. 1 a, the typical transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of CQDs revealed that they were well-dispersed and uniform. The HRTEM image of a single CQD particle in the inset showed that the crystal lattice spacing was ∼0.320nm consistent with the (002) lattice planes of graphitic carbon. Through measuring about 200 particles, we calculated the size distribution of CQDs in Fig. 1b, which displayed approximately 5.35±0.42nm of the average size. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs was recorded and showed in the black line of Fig. 1c. It can be clearly seen that CQDs showed a typical optical absorption peak at about 250nm. The red trace in Fig. 1c showed the Raman spectrum of CQDs. Two typical characteristic peaks were located at ∼1336 and ∼1607cm−1 which were attributed to the D- and G-band of carbon, respectively.

The CQDs are full of functional groups (hydroxyl and carboxyl groups) on their surface and fragmented graphitic structure with no further modification [25,30]. From the previous literature reported by our group, the base titration could be used to quantify the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of the water-soluble CQDs (∼5nm) [25,31]. Fig. 1d showed that the relative content of hydroxyl versus carboxyl groups of the CQDs was about 7:1, which was determined by conductometric titration. The detailed experimental information was shown in the Supplementary information and Table S2.

The morphology and structure of the products based on typical CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 (Sn:Co=1:3), aliased as CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4, were characterized by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Figs. 2 a and S1, the TEM images under different magnifications suggested that the CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 sample was lamellar-like structure. The scanning transmission electron microscope (STEM) and the corresponding elemental mapping images were recorded in Fig. 2b, where C, O, Sn and Co elements were detected. The enlarged TEM image and the FFT pattern in Fig. 2c revealed that the SnO2, Co3O4 and CQDs (∼5nm) successfully formed a composite. The HRTEM image (Fig. 2d) of the CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 composite showed that there were three kinds of lattice fringes with the different lattice spacing, and the insoluble CQDs layer was on the surface of SnO2. The fringe spacing of ∼0.447nm agreed well with the spacing of the (111) lattice plane of Co3O4 [32], the lattice fringe with an interplanar spacing of ∼0.320nm was ascribed to the (002) crystallo-graphic planes of graphitic carbon [27,28] and the fringe spacing of 0.261nm attributed to the (101) planes of SnO2 nanocrystallites [16], which was the strong supporting evidence of the formation of CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 composite. The energy-dispersive X-ray spectrum (EDS) analysis (Fig. S2) further confirmed the formation of the CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 compound.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured to analyze chemical state of different elements in the composite. The XPS survey spectrum of the CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 composite in Fig. 3 a showed the C, O, Co and Sn peaks. The C1s spectrum could be divided into four peaks in Fig. 3b. The peaks at 284.8 and 286.5eV were assigned to C–C and C–O/C–O–C bonding, and peaks at 287.9 and 289.0eV were attributed to OC and OC–O bonding, respectively [30,36,41]. Compared to the pure CQDs C1s spectrum (Fig. S3), those peaks in Fig. 3b further confirmed that the carbon in CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 was from the CQDs. As shown in Fig. 3c, the high resolution XPS spectrum of Co 2p showed two obvious peaks observed at around 780.4eV and 796.7eV, characterized of Co 2p1/2 and Co 2p3/2 of Co3O4, respectively [37,38]. The high resolution XPS spectra of the Sn 3d were presented in Fig. 3d, where the Sn 3d5/2 and Sn 3d3/2 peak at 486.9eV and 495.4eV, respectively [39,40]. From the Raman spectra of CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 shown in Fig. S5, the G-band at 1576cm−1 and the D-band at 1326cm−1 were observed in CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 (black line). Compared to those observed in the Raman spectra of CQDs (Fig. 1c), there was a slight shift, indicating that CQDs was the source of carbon. The Raman peaks at 490cm−1 and 688cm−1 could be ascribed to the F2g and A1g modes of Co3O4, respectively [41,42]. These mentioned characterizations above suggested the successful synthesis of CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 composite.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the as-prepared pure SnO2, pure Co3O4, CQDs, SnO2–Co3O4 and CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 samples were shown in Fig. 4 a. All the characteristic diffraction peaks marked by ‘‘#” in the black line could be identified as the various crystalline planes of SnO2 (JCPDS No. 41-1445) [33]. In the red line, all the characteristic peaks marked by ‘‘&” were fit for the Co3O4 standard pattern well (JCPDS No. 65–3103) [32,34]. From the green line of SnO2–Co3O4, it could be clearly seen that the diffraction peak at 60.9° agreed well with the (511) lattice planes of Co3O4 and the peaks at 34.3° and 51.9° existed in the pure SnO2 pattern, which confirmed the formation of SnO2–Co3O4 composite. The XRD pattern of the CQDs was shown in the blue line. For the pattern of CQDs, a broad peak centered at 23.4° (signed with *), which was related to the disordered carbon [21]. In the pink line of CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4, the XRD pattern showed no evident crystalline structure change compared to the SnO2–Co3O4 sample but an increase of intensity of the peak at 34.30°, which was assigned to (101) lattice planes. Maybe due to the relatively low diffraction intensity and the small amount of carbon in the CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 composite, the characteristic peaks for carbon were hardly to be observed.

Fig. 4b showed the FT-IR spectrum of the pure CQDs and the CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 composite catalyst. For the black line of pure CQDs, several peaks were located at approximately 3447 (O–H), 1631 (CO), 1403 (CC), and 1243 (C–OH)cm−1 [30]. These peaks in the CQDs spectrum agreed with some new functional groups and the oxygen-containing groups, which manifested the formation of hydrophilic groups and a successful oxidation of graphite. In the red line of CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4, the observed broad vibration bands at 551 and 725–1000cm−1 may be associated to the metal–oxygen (Co–O or Sn–O modes) stretching vibration [35]. Compared with pure CQDs, the CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 composite showed a slight shift of some dominant peaks, for example, the CO peak shifted ∼69cm−1 because of the strong electrostatic interactions between SnO2, Co3O4 and CQDs species. Based on the important results, we could suggest that CQDs here not only show physical absorption with SnO2–Co3O4 but also have additional strong interactions with the pristine materials. Fig. S4 showed the PL spectra of the CQDs, SnO2–Co3O4 and CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 composite catalysts. Compared with CQDs, there was an apparent decay of PL intensity for CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4. Hence, we could conclude that electron transfers existed between CQDs and SnO2–Co3O4.

The electrocatalytic properties for OER of the CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 composite with various Sn:Co mole ratios were investigated by the potential at a current density of 2mAcm−1 and the current density at 0.8V in 0.1M KOH solution. Fig. 5 a showed the results of the catalytic activities of the series of CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 samples. The CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 samples with different contents of Sn and Co showed various OER activity based on the potentials and current density. When the initial Sn:Co molar ratio was 1:3, the catalyst exhibited the best performance with a lower potential and a higher current density. Fig. 5b showed the LSV curves of CQDs, 20% Pt/C, SnO2, Co3O4, SnO2–Co3O4 and CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 (the latter two with Sn:Co=1:3), respectively as electrocatalysts in 0.1M KOH solution. The bare RDE gave no observed current or oxygen evolution, even when the potential was increased to 0.8 V. However, the electrode modified with CQDs-based sample CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 (Sn:Co=1:3) exhibited observably oxygen evolution at 0.562V, followed by the fleetly increasing of current under more positive potentials. The electrocatalytic activities toward OER for these samples increased in the sequence of SnO2 <CQDs<Co3O4 <SnO2–Co3O4 <CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4. This fact demonstrated that the OER catalytic activity of CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 was improved greatly according to the more negative potential and bigger current density via regulating the proportion of Sn and Co. Meanwhile, the CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 sample with a ratio of 1:3 (Sn/Co) showed the best electrochemical activity based on a high current density at 0.8 V and a negative onset potential.

For direct comparison, CV curves of the CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 (Sn:Co=1:3), CQDs, SnO2, Co3O4, SnO2–Co3O4 (Sn:Co=1:3) and the bare glassy carbon electrode were measured to investigate their catalytic activity for OER under the same condition in Fig. 6 a. The CV curves further revealed that the onset potential of CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 (Sn:Co=1:3) was the most negative compared with other catalysts. Fig. 6b showed the O2 production during ten sequential CV scans between 0 and 0.8V, applied at the glassy carbon electrode modified with the CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 (Sn:Co=1:3) catalyst. Ten subsequent current transients were recorded, which exhibited a similar anodic performance on multicycle activity. The observation emphasized the extreme robustness of the hybrid CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 (Sn:Co=1:3) nanocomposite, resulting in retention of the electrocatalytic activities and an excellent structural stability. The bare GC showed no electrochemical activity toward oxygen evolution reaction under the same condition (Fig. 6b, blue line).

To further investigate the electrocatalytic activity of the CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 catalyst for OER, LSV measurement was conducted on the glassy carbon electrodes modified with the same catalyst in 0.1 and 1M KOH solutions. Fig. 7 a showed that CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 held an excellent OER activity. In 0.1M KOH, the CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 catalyst showed an onset potential of OER at ∼0.56V; similarly, the onset potential of the composite considerably reduced to ∼0.51V in 1M KOH. Steady-state Tafel measurements for the CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 composite were shown in Fig. 7b. The CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 catalyst exhibited a Tafel slope of ≈62mV/decade in 0.1M KOH and ≈60mV/decade in 1M KOH, which were lower than that of previous reports about Co3O4-based OER catalysts under the same conditions [43].

According to the results suggested above, we could see that the CQDs efficiently enhanced the electrocatalytic performance and durability of SnO2–Co3O4 due to the interaction and bonding between the CQDs, SnO2 and Co3O4. In order to further confirm that the CQDs played a crucial influence on the excellent catalytic OER activity of the composite, we introduced three different sizes of nanocarbon (∼5nm, as CQDs, ∼10nm, and ∼100nm) cooperated with SnO2 and Co3O4 (Sn:Co=1:3) under the same conditions for comparison. The polarization curves of the as-prepared samples were recorded with a scan rate of 10mVs−1 in 0.1M KOH solution (Fig. 8 a). The electrocatalytic activities of the composites decreased obviously with the size of nanocarbon increasing. Under a proper condition, the CQDs (∼5nm)/SnO2–Co3O4 (Sn:Co=1:3) composite showed the largest current and the most negative onset potential for OER. The SnO2–Co3O4 composite with ∼10nm carbon has an onset potential of ∼0.59V, and the value for ∼100nm carbon decreased to a value even lower than that of the SnO2–Co3O4 sample. Thus, we could conclude that CQDs cooperated with SnO2 and Co3O4 improved the electrocatalytic activity toward OER significantly. After that, we carried out the current–time (i–t) chronoamperometric response measurements of 6h for three composites formed with SnO2, Co3O4 (Sn:Co=1:3) and different sizes of nanocarbon (∼5, ∼10 and ∼100nm) at 0.57V (vs. SCE) toward OER. As shown in Fig. 8b, the SnO2–Co3O4 composite with ∼5nm carbon dots showed tiny loss with a high relative current of 93.5% over 6h, but the nanocarbons (∼10nm and ∼100nm)/SnO2–Co3O4 (Sn:Co=1:3) composite catalysts only retained 85.7% and 78.5% of the original current within the same time. These results suggested that the composite formed with SnO2 and Co3O4 (Sn:Co=1:3) and ∼5nm nanocarbon held the best stability performance in an alkaline medium. This may be accounted for more functional groups in unit area of pony-size carbon dots, which facilitated the combination with SnO2 and Co3O4. Furthermore, a larger specific surface area of the small size carbon dots was more favorable to form the protective layer of the composite, leading to the better stability.

To further verify the speculation, we also prepared hydroxyl-group-free CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4, graphite/SnO2–Co3O4, carbon nanotube/SnO2–Co3O4 and graphene/SnO2–Co3O4 composite for electrochemical water oxidation and conducted the stability tests for 6h (the TEM images of hydroxyl-group-free CQDs, graphite, carbon nanotube and graphene see Fig. S6). Compared to the FT-IR spectra of the above four catalysts (in Fig. S7), the CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 catalyst showed more abundant functional groups (Fig. 4b). In Fig. S8, the CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 catalyst maintained the highest relative current after 6h, demonstrating the long-term stability. The decreasing OER activity of other catalysts may be attributed to a small mass loss of the catalyst due to the unsteady structure. CQDs with abundant functional groups on the surface facilitated the interaction with SnO2 and Co3O4 to form a stabilized composite. The long-term stable electrochemical catalytic activity was vital for one catalyst in high concentration alkaline solutions in applications. On the basis of the results above, we suggested that besides excellent OER activity, the CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 composite electrocatalyst also exhibited long-term cyclic stability in alkaline solutions. In addition, compared to the common OER catalysts (Ir and Ru oxides, etc.), the present CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 composite catalyst is inexpensive, earth-abundant and easily constructed. Our founding could be beneficial to resolve the problems in preparation of catalysts for OER, which may vastly push the research in current energy technology and other related areas.

Based on the results and discussions above, we could explain the improved electrochemical activity and superb cyclic stability of the CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 complex catalysts as follows. Firstly, the high electrocatalytic property was primarily attributed to the Co3O4. Co atoms were identified as the active centers for OER in the CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 composite. The insoluble CQDs layer on the surface of SnO2 could efficiently protect SnO2 from dissolution in alkaline solution for a long time, thus the structural stability of CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 could be greatly enhanced during the electrocatalytic process. On the other hand, the SnO2 formed beside Co3O4 offered the possibility of combining good electronic conductivity of the carbon, which facilitated the direct electron communication between the active sites of the catalyst and the electrode surfaces and increased the current density. At the same time, CQDs could be regarded as both electron acceptors and donors, in which the electrons could be readily transferred to the surface of Co3O4 (as shown in Fig. 9 ). Finally, the small size of CQDs could provide a large specific surface area for convenient electrocatalytic reaction, and the rapid electron transfer on the surface could further improve the electrocatalytic activity. The functional groups on the surface of CQDs (such as carboxys) made the formation of CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 composites easier because of the strong electrostatic interactions between SnO2, Co3O4 and CQDs. These above factors together accounted for the enhanced electrocatalytic activity and good stability of CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 electrocatalysts toward OER. Herein, compared with some other nanocarbon materials which might need further modification before use, it should be pointed out that CQDs with abundant functional groups and unique physicochemical properties were easier to design composite materials. Moreover, CQDs might also enhance the electrocatalytic performance with proper surface modifications or doping with non-metallic elements, such as N, B, P, and S. Considering the versatility and diversity of the structural design in the CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 composite system, the synergistic coupling of CQDs, SnO2 and Co3O4 as a favorable catalyst may offer a new channel to design high effective CQDs-based electrocatalysts for a wide range of practical applications.

We presented the design and synthesis of CQDs/SnO2–Co3O4 composites with different Co3O4 and SnO2 contents via a facile hydrothermal method, among which the electrocatalyst with molar ratio of Sn:Co ratio of 1:3 revealed the highest catalytic activity. The inexpensive, earth-abundant, and easily constructed catalyst showed excellent OER electrochemical activity with negative onset potential and large anodic current density. Moreover, the covering layer of insoluble CQDs on the surface protected the catalyst availably, resulting in a stable structure and long-term stability in alkaline medium. We further provided new insight into designing and fabricating effective electrocatalysts for oxygen evolution reaction by combining non-precious functional materials with CQDs, which could be contributed to address the energy and environmental problems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology, the National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program) (2012CB825803, 2013CB932702), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51422207, 51132006, 21471106), the Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education (20123201110018), a Suzhou Planning Project of Science and Technology (ZXG2012028), and a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2015.03.002.
